ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general sushi class (FOODON_00002678) but no specific inari sushi subclass. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP covers sushi as a category. No specific inari sushi code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept for inari sushi identified in the SNOMED CT food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of inari sushi is 3 pieces, each consisting of a seasoned aburaage tofu pouch filled with vinegared sushi rice, weighing approximately 150 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Inari-zushi (Inari sushi) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; cross-referenced with Japanese Standard Tables of Food Composition (MEXT 2020) entry for inarizushi
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology Japan (MEXT)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary values adapted from USDA FoodData Central NDB entries for sushi rice and aburaage, cross-referenced with MEXT Japanese Food Composition Tables 2020 (8th edition) entry for inarizushi (item code 18009). Per-100 g values represent the composite dish as typically prepared: seasoned aburaage pouch filled with vinegared sushi rice.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for sushi rice / Japanese rice dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for inari sushi was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 55 is estimated from the closest analogous food: white sushi rice (GI ~55, low category) as reported in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) and the University of Sydney GI Database. The aburaage component contributes fat and protein which moderately lower the overall GI relative to plain rice. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (55 × 30.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.5 serving size factor) / 100 = approximately 25.2.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoned aburaage
hypertension.guidance: Inari sushi contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 465 mg per 3-piece serving), primarily from soy sauce used to season the aburaage pouch; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or pickled ginger.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index from white sushi rice but moderate to high glycemic load from carbohydrate content and added sugar in aburaage seasoning
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 55 (low category) but a glycemic load of approximately 25 per 3-piece serving (moderate to high), inari sushi can still cause a significant postprandial glucose rise due to its carbohydrate content; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to 1–2 pieces per meal and pair with low-GI foods or non-starchy vegetables to manage blood glucose effectively.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat with moderate sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Inari sushi is relatively low in saturated fat (approximately 0.7 g per 100 g) and contains no cholesterol, making it a heart-friendly option in moderate portions; however, the sodium content warrants attention for individuals managing cardiovascular risk through dietary sodium restriction.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Inari sushi (いなり寿司, inarizushi) is a traditional Japanese sushi variety consisting of vinegared sushi rice stuffed inside a seasoned, sweet-savory deep-fried tofu pouch called aburaage. The aburaage is simmered in a mixture of dashi, soy sauce, mirin, and sugar until it absorbs the seasoning and becomes tender, lightly sweet, and umami-rich. The rice filling is the same short-grain Japanese sushi rice used in other sushi styles, seasoned with rice vinegar, sugar, and salt. The resulting piece has a soft, slightly chewy exterior from the tofu pouch and a firm, slightly sticky rice interior. Inari sushi is named after the Shinto deity Inari, whose messenger is the fox (kitsune), and aburaage is said to be the fox's favourite food. It is one of the most popular and accessible forms of sushi in Japan, widely sold in convenience stores, supermarkets, and sushi restaurants. In Singapore, inari sushi is commonly served at Japanese restaurant chains such as Sushi Express. A standard serving is 3 pieces (approximately 150 g). Nutritionally, inari sushi provides moderate energy (approximately 185 kcal per 100 g) from carbohydrates in the rice and fat from the tofu pouch. It is a source of plant-based protein from tofu (approximately 5.2 g per 100 g) and contains polyunsaturated fats from the aburaage. The sodium content is moderate to high due to soy sauce seasoning. It is suitable for vegetarians but not vegans due to the use of dashi (fish stock) in traditional preparation. Clinically, the low GI but moderate to high glycemic load require portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, and the sodium content is relevant for those managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: How many calories in inari sushi?
What is the tofu pouch in sushi called?
How much sodium is in inari sushi?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central and cross-referenced with MEXT Japanese Food Composition Tables 2020 for inarizushi. No exact single-source match was found in an approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo does not list inari sushi specifically). GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (white sushi rice) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Serving size set to 150 g (3 pieces) consistent with the input description '3pcs Inari Sushi' and typical restaurant portion. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed zero as aburaage is a plant-based product with negligible trans fat per USDA data. Cholesterol is 0.0 — confirmed zero as inari sushi contains no animal-derived fat in the aburaage or rice components. Vitamin C is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this dish contains no significant vitamin C sources. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal products contributing B12. Vitamin A (mcg RAE) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted source for this composite dish. Vitamin B6 is -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted source. Folate (mcg DFE) is -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted source. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted source. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted source. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted source. Water_g is -1 as it is not reported in the primary adapted source. is_vegan set to false because traditional inarizushi uses dashi (fish-based stock) in aburaage seasoning; vegetarian versions using kombu dashi exist but are not the default. is_halal set to false as standard preparation uses mirin (contains alcohol) and may use non-halal certified soy sauce; halal-certified versions are available at some Singapore outlets. GI source is an analogous estimate based on white sushi rice GI of 55 from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); no direct inari sushi GI measurement was found in approved sources. GI of 55 is classified as 'low'.
